flashcards.front: What is Process Scheduling in OS?
flashcards.back: Process scheduling is the OS mechanism that decides which process runs on the CPU at any given time, managing the queue of ready processes to optimize CPU utilization and system performance.

flashcards.front: Define CPU Burst and I/O Burst
flashcards.back: CPU Burst is the time a process spends executing on the CPU. I/O Burst is the time a process spends waiting for I/O operations. A process alternates between these bursts throughout its lifecycle.

flashcards.front: What is Waiting Time in scheduling?
flashcards.back: Waiting Time is the total time a process spends in the ready queue waiting for CPU allocation. It is calculated as Turnaround Time minus Burst Time.
Turnaround Time is the total time from process arrival to completion. Formula: Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time.
Throughput is the number of processes completed per unit time. Higher throughput indicates better system efficiency.
Context switching is the process of saving and restoring CPU state when switching between processes. It involves saving registers, program counter, and other process control blocks.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Preemptive and Non-preemptive scheduling?
flashcards.back: Preemptive scheduling allows the OS to forcefully remove CPU from a running process (based on priority or time slice). Non-preemptive scheduling lets a process run to completion or until it voluntarily yields CPU.

flashcards.front: What is Convoy Effect in FCFS?
flashcards.back: Convoy Effect occurs in FCFS when short processes get stuck behind long processes, causing poor CPU utilization and longer average waiting time.

flashcards.front: What is Aging in process scheduling?
flashcards.back: Aging is a technique where the priority of a waiting process increases with its wait time in the ready queue, preventing starvation of long-waiting processes.
Response Time is the time from process arrival to when the CPU executes the process for the first time. Critical for interactive systems.
Round Robin assigns a fixed time slice (quantum) to each process in a cyclic manner. Processes are placed in a circular queue and each gets equal CPU time.

flashcards.front: What is SJF (Shortest Job First) scheduling?
flashcards.back: SJF selects the process with the shortest burst time from the ready queue. It can be preemptive (SRTF) or non-preemptive, minimizing average waiting time.
Priority Scheduling executes processes based on their priority value. Higher priority processes run first. Can suffer from starvation of low-priority processes.
Multilevel Queue divides ready queue into separate queues for different process types (system, interactive, batch). Each queue has its own scheduling algorithm.

flashcards.front: What is Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling?
flashcards.back: MLFQ allows processes to move between queues based on their CPU burst behavior. Processes using too much CPU are moved to lower priority queues, preventing starvation.
